eli3: Continuous distributions describe uncountable outcomes: height, weight, time, temperature. Instead of P(X = x), we use probability density functions (PDF) where P(a ≤ X ≤ b) is the area under the curve between a and b.

Key distributions: Normal/Gaussian (bell curve, central to statistics), Exponential (waiting times, memoryless), Uniform (equal probability over range), Beta (probabilities, bounded [0,1]), Gamma (positive, flexible shapes).
The total area under the PDF equals 1. Individual points have probability 0; only intervals have positive probability. The cumulative distribution function (CDF) gives P(X ≤ x) as area from -∞ to x.

whenWhy.title: Normal distribution and Central Limit Theorem
whenWhy.body: Sums of many small independent effects tend to Normal (CLT). Measurement errors, heights, test scores often approximately Normal. Linear regression assumes Normal errors. Kalman filters use Normal distributions for state estimation.

whenWhy.title: Bayesian inference with Beta and Gamma
whenWhy.body: Beta distribution models probabilities (conjugate to Binomial). Gamma models positive values (conjugate to Poisson). Dirichlet generalizes Beta to multiple categories. Used extensively in Bayesian ML and A/B testing.

whenWhy.title: Survival analysis and reliability
whenWhy.body: Exponential: constant hazard rate (memoryless). Weibull: flexible hazard (increasing, decreasing, or constant). Used in reliability engineering, medical survival analysis, customer churn prediction.

whenWhy.title: Generative models and VAEs
whenWhy.body: Variational autoencoders use Normal distributions for latent spaces. Normalizing flows transform simple distributions (Normal) to complex ones via invertible mappings. Diffusion models gradually add/remove Gaussian noise.

deepDiveIntro: A continuous random variable is described by a probability density function — a curve where the area under it between two values equals the probability of falling in that interval. The total area under the curve must be exactly 1. The cumulative distribution function gives the probability of being at or below any value, and its derivative gives back the density. The mean is a weighted average of all possible values, with the density as weights.

When you apply a function to a random variable to get a new one, the density of the result is related to the original density by the change-of-variables formula, which accounts for how the function stretches or compresses the number line locally.

deepDive.heading: Normal (Gaussian) Distribution
deepDive.body: The Normal distribution is the classic symmetric bell curve, fully described by its mean and standard deviation. The empirical rule gives three useful benchmarks: about 68% of values fall within one standard deviation of the mean, 95% within two, and 99.7% within three. The Central Limit Theorem says that sums of many independent random variables converge to Normal, which is why it appears everywhere in statistics.

deepDive.heading: Exponential Distribution
deepDive.body: The Exponential distribution models waiting times when the chance of an event happening in any small interval is constant — for example, the time until the next customer arrives or the next server failure occurs. Its defining feature is memorylessness: knowing you have already waited a certain amount of time gives you no information about how much longer you will wait.

deepDive.heading: Gamma Distribution
deepDive.body: The Gamma distribution is a flexible family for positive-valued data with a shape parameter and a rate parameter. It includes the Exponential (shape = 1) and the chi-squared distribution as special cases. It is commonly used for modelling waiting times, rainfall amounts, and as a conjugate prior for Poisson rate parameters in Bayesian analysis.

deepDive.heading: Beta Distribution
deepDive.body: The Beta distribution lives on the interval from 0 to 1, making it natural for modelling probabilities, proportions, and rates. Its shape is controlled by two parameters — values above 1 create a unimodal hump, values below 1 create a U-shape, and equal values of 1 give a flat uniform distribution. It is the conjugate prior for the Binomial likelihood, making Bayesian updating on coin-flip type data analytically simple.

deepDive.heading: Log-Normal Distribution
deepDive.body: A variable is Log-Normal when its logarithm is normally distributed. Log-Normal variables are always positive and right-skewed — a small number of very large values pull the mean well above the median. It arises naturally from multiplicative processes: just as sums of many independent variables converge to Normal, products of many positive independent variables converge to Log-Normal. Income distributions, stock prices, and file sizes are classic examples.

takeaways: PDF f(x): P(a ≤ X ≤ b) = area under f from a to b.
Normal: bell curve, symmetric, foundation of CLT.
Gamma: flexible positive distribution; Exponential and Chi-squared are special cases.
Beta: [0,1] support, models probabilities, conjugate to Binomial.

edgeCases.q: Can continuous random variables take any real value?
edgeCases.a: Theoretically yes, but specific distributions have restricted support: Exponential and Gamma are x > 0; Beta is x ∈ [0,1]; Normal is all ℝ. Probability is 0 outside the support. In practice, numerical precision limits the range.

edgeCases.q: Why is P(X = x) = 0 for continuous variables?
edgeCases.a: Probability is area under PDF. A single point has zero width, hence zero area. This is why we use PDF (density, not probability) and compute probabilities over intervals. CDF F(x) = P(X ≤ x) is well-defined and continuous.

edgeCases.q: When should I use Gamma vs Log-Normal?
edgeCases.a: Both model positive, right-skewed data. Gamma is more flexible with shape parameter and has conjugate priors. Log-Normal arises naturally from multiplicative processes (products of variables) and is appropriate when log-transformed data is Normal. In practice, often similar fits; choose based on theoretical justification or model fit.

sources.label: Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 3
Forbes et al. — Statistical Distributions (4th ed.)
